title: Cramping pain with vomiting and no stool

chart.oneLiner: 60F with prior abdominal surgery presents with a day of crampy abdominal pain, distension, bilious vomiting, and inability to pass stool or gas.

opening: My belly's all crampy and swollen, I keep throwing up, and I haven't been able to go to the bathroom.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 60-year-old woman, uncomfortable in waves (colicky), abdomen visibly distended. Cooperative.

HPI: ~24h of crampy, intermittent (colicky) abdominal pain that comes in waves, progressive abdominal distension, multiple episodes of vomiting (now bilious), and OBSTIPATION — no flatus or stool since yesterday. Prior open hysterectomy years ago (adhesions; only if asked about surgeries). NO fever initially, NO bloody stool.
PMH: hysterectomy (open), hypertension. MEDS: amlodipine. SOCIAL: nonsmoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable, mild dehydration.
- Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 104, RR 18, 37.4°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Abdomen: distended, tympanitic; diffuse mild tenderness without focal peritonism early; high-pitched/tinkling bowel sounds (or becoming absent); well-healed midline surgical scar. No hernias palpable (check groin).
- Mucous membranes dry.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the prior surgery unless asked about past operations or scars.
- Bowel sounds, distension, and the scar are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BMP: Na 134, K 3.3, Cl 92, HCO3 30 — hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis from vomiting; BUN 28, Cr 1.2 (prerenal).
labs.Lactate: 1.6 — normal (a rising lactate would raise concern for strangulation/ischemia).
labs.CT_abdomen: CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated proximal small-bowel loops with a transition point and collapsed distal bowel; findings consistent with adhesive small bowel obstruction; no free air or pneumatosis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm; possible changes from hypokalemia (flattened T waves / U waves); no acute ischemia.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Hypokalaemia' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Sinus rhythm; possible changes from hypokalemia (flattened T waves / U waves); no acute is … and self-host with credit to litfl.com.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead EKG in sinus rhythm with hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves and U waves)
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 65-year-old woman with prolonged vomiting reports fatigue and palpitations after poor oral intake.

cxr.label: Abdominal/chest X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Multiple dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels in a step-ladder pattern; no free air under the diaphragm.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC plain film showing dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels (SBO); must NOT show free air.
cxr.imageDescription: upright abdominal radiograph showing dilated small-bowel loops with step-ladder air-fluid levels
searchTerms: small bowel obstruction air-fluid levels x-ray
dilated small bowel loops step-ladder upright film
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 74-year-old man presents with crampy abdominal pain, distension, and absence of flatus.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Small bowel obstruction (leading,8; accepts bowel obstruction). Adynamic ileus (core,3). Large bowel obstruction (core,3). Strangulation/ischemia (CANT-MISS,4). Incarcerated hernia (core,2). Volvulus (bonus,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — colicky pain, distension, obstipation, prior surgery (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Small bowel obstruction. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Strangulation/ischemia. Also reasonable: Adynamic ileus; Large bowel obstruction; Incarcerated hernia.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — colicky pain, distension, obstipation, prior surgery

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Upright abdominal/chest X-ray (CANT-MISS,4). CT abdomen/pelvis to confirm and find transition point (CANT-MISS,4). BMP (3). CBC (2). Lactate to assess for ischemia (2). PENALTY: shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; BMP; CBC; Lactate; CT abdomen/pelvis.

criticalActions.item: CT abdomen/pelvis to confirm and find transition point

requiredTests: CT abdomen/pelvis to confirm and find transition point

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus rhythm; possible hypokalemic changes (flat T/U waves); no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (6); no acute ischemic changes (4); recognizes possible electrolyte (hypokalemia) effect (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes possible hypokalemic effect (flat T waves / U waves)
criticalFindings: Sinus rhythm; possible changes from hypokalemia (flattened T waves / U waves); no acute ischemia.
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm; possible changes from hypokalemia (flattened T waves / U waves); no acute ischemia.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus rhythm; possible changes from hypokalemia (flattened T waves / U waves); no acute ischemia.

coreActions.item: recognizes possible hypokalemic effect (flat T waves / U waves)

steps.label: Read X-ray
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's upright film. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels (step-ladder); no free air. CREDIT: identifies dilated bowel loops with air-fluid levels (5); recognizes the obstructive pattern (small bowel) (3); excludes free air (perforation) (2).
keyFindings: identifies dilated bowel loops with air-fluid levels
criticalFindings: Multiple dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels in a step-ladder pattern; no free air under the diaphragm.
commonMistakes: mistaking dilated small bowel for large bowel
missing free air under the diaphragm
steps.idealAnswer: Multiple dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels in a step-ladder pattern; no free air under the diaphragm.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Multiple dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels in a step-ladder pattern; no free air under the diaphragm.

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the CT, what is your diagnosis and what raises concern for the bowel being compromised?
steps.rubric: Adhesive small bowel obstruction (5). Notes a transition point with proximal dilation/distal collapse (3). Flags strangulation warning signs — rising lactate, fever, peritonitis, pneumatosis — that mandate surgery (bonus,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Adhesive small bowel obstruction — CT shows dilated proximal small-bowel loops with a transition point and collapsed distal bowel (likely from prior surgical adhesions). Concern for bowel compromise/strangulation is raised by a rising lactate, fever, peritonitis, or pneumatosis, any of which mandates urgent surgery.

bonusActions.item: Flags strangulation warning signs — rising lactate, fever, peritonitis, pneumatosis — that mandate surgery

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH abdominal pain (obstruction): NPO + NG-tube decompression (CANT-MISS,6); IV fluid resuscitation + electrolyte correction (5); surgery consult (5); analgesia/antiemetics (3); urgent surgery if strangulation/ischemia/closed-loop (4); serial exams (2). PENALTY: feeding / no decompression in obstruction (-3); missing signs of strangulation (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: NPO, nasogastric tube decompression, IV fluid resuscitation, correct electrolytes. Analgesia and antiemetics. Surgery consult. Non-operative trial for partial/adhesive SBO; urgent surgery for strangulation/ischemia/closed-loop or failure to resolve. Serial exams and imaging.

bonusActions.item: Trial of non-operative management if uncomplicated

optionalActions: Trial of non-operative management if uncomplicated

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 60F with prior abdominal surgery presents with a day of crampy abdominal pain, distension, bilious vomiting, and inability to pass stool or gas. The diagnosis is Small bowel obstruction, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: distended, tympanitic; diffuse mild tenderness without focal peritonism early; high-pitched/tinkling bowel sounds (or becoming absent); well-healed midline surgical scar. No hernias palpable (check groin).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Small bowel obstruction while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Small bowel obstruction and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
